title: Why most trend-following strategies fail in crypto (even though they work in theory)
author: Witalson
selftext:
Trend-following is probably one of the most common approaches in crypto trading.

On paper, it makes perfect sense:

* follow the trend
* ride momentum
* exit when the trend weakens

And using tools like moving averages, breakouts, or ATR — it actually works pretty well…

**but only in trending markets.**

The problem starts when the market goes sideways.

In those conditions, most trend-following systems:

* generate false signals
* get stopped out repeatedly
* accumulate small losses

Over time, this “chop” eats into performance.

So it feels like the issue isn’t really the strategy itself — but how it behaves across different market conditions.

What’s interesting is that more advanced systems try to solve this not by improving the signal, but by improving **execution and risk handling**.

For example:

* running multiple strategies instead of relying on one
* scaling out of positions instead of closing all at once
* using trailing stops instead of fixed exits
* adjusting position size depending on market conditions

Especially the last one seems underrated.

If the system reduces exposure during low volatility and increases it during strong trends, the whole performance profile changes.

👉 small losses during choppy markets  
👉 larger gains when real trends appear

Which basically means a few good trades can offset a lot of noise.

Curious how others here approach this:

Do you focus more on improving signals — or on execution and risk adaptation?
